text: In today's video, we will be describing laparoscopic in-bag cystectomy for a dermoid cyst. This procedure avoids any spillage of content into the peritoneal cavity.
The endobag is introduced into the abdomen and rolled open before being positioned in the pelvis. The ovary containing the dermoid cyst is then placed inside the endobag.
The trick of this entire surgery is to be extremely gentle during the entire surgical procedure.
The laparoscopic view reveals the left ovary with a dermoid cyst measuring approximately four by three centimeters.
The surgeon uses cold knife scissors to create a gentle plane of dissection over the ovarian cortex at its most prominent point, aiming to open the cyst wall.
The surgeon gently opens the cyst wall using a laparoscopic shearer, extending the incision to facilitate the removal of the dermoid cyst from the ovary.

The surgeon performs enucleation of the dermoid cyst from the ovarian cortex using gentle traction and counter traction, ensuring the cyst does not rupture or spill its contents outside the endobag. The cystectomy is carried out along the correct plane of dissection.
The entire trick is to perform the cystectomy inside the endo bag.
The surgeon has completed most of the dissection of the dermoid cyst from the left ovary, with the surgical field appearing bloodless.
The surgeon spills the cyst contents open inside the endo bag to prevent spillage into the peritoneum, then uses suction aspiration to remove the contents from the bag.
After aspirating the cyst contents, the surgeon completes the cystectomy by carefully removing the remaining cyst wall from the surrounding tissue within the endo bag.
After completing the cystectomy, the surgeon examines the ovarian bed for any signs of bleeding or spurting vessels.
The small cyst is visible inside the endobag and is being retrieved from the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon uses a 10 mm suprapubic port to perform a gentle peritoneal lavage and re-aspirate any spilled contents. The ovarian bed is shown with complete hemostasis, without any visible bleeding.
